Summary: Social Media - LinkedIn - Webinar diagnostic poll: which measurement gap eats most of your week?
Description:
Topic: Diagnostic poll on which measurement gap eats most of the IC team's week — preview for the Rachel Folz webinar on Insights / Measurement (2026-06-25)
Post type: poll
Wraps: rachel-folz-june-2026
Copy:
[CAPTION]

The Insights / Measurement webinar on June 25 opens with a diagnostic. Before Rachel walks through the May 2026 Insights redesign, attendees self-sort by where their measurement work is breaking down.

Most IC teams have one of four gaps eating the week. Open rate as the only number leadership sees, so it gets treated as the proof point even when it isn't one. Reports that lump every employee together, so the team can't say what landed with the frontline vs the corporate office. Channel-by-channel dashboards that don't talk to each other, so a Teams post and an email blast each take credit for the same employee. Or no through-line from a send to a behavior, so when a CHRO asks "did the policy update land," the honest answer is "we sent it."

All four are real problems. Most weeks one of them is the one slowing the team down.

Which gap is yours this quarter? Webinar registration in the first comment.

[POLL]

Question: Which measurement gap eats most of your IC week?
Options:
1. Open rate (the vanity number)
2. No audience segmentation in reports
3. No cross-channel attribution
4. No through-line from send to behavior
Duration: 1 week

[HASHTAGS]

#InternalCommunications #EmployeeEngagement #Measurement

Summary: Social Media - LinkedIn - Webinar promo theme: Insights / Measurement with Rachel Folz (6/25) — registration push
Description:
Topic: Registration push for the Rachel Folz Insights / Measurement webinar (2026-06-25, 12:00 PM EDT). Positioning the May 2026 Insights redesign as the measurement layer ICs need to defend their work to leadership.
Post type: static-theme
Wraps: rachel-folz-june-2026
Copy:
[CAPTION]

Most IC teams can tell you the open rate. They can't tell you who acted on the message.

When a CHRO asks "did the new benefits policy land with the frontline," the answer most teams have to give is "we sent it, and 38% opened." That isn't the question.

Open rate is the easiest number to produce and the easiest to defend, so it became the default report. Useful, but not enough to hold the function's ground when the budget conversation comes around.

The measurement layer IC teams need ties a send to a segment, a segment to an outcome, and an outcome to a number the CHRO can put in a board update. Per-audience, per-channel, per-content. With the receipts the compliance team also asks for.

The May 2026 Insights redesign in Broadcast was rebuilt around that question. Rachel Folz, our Head of Product, is walking through it on June 25 at 12 PM ET.

Bring the dashboard your CHRO last asked for. She'll show you how to build it.

45 minutes, free, no card. Link in comments.

[ASSET SPEC]

Visual concept: Speaker card. Rachel Folz headshot, name, title (Head of Product, Cerkl), webinar title ("Insights / Measurement"), and date/time block ("June 25 · 12 PM ET").
Asset role: speaker-card from rachel-folz-june-2026/canva-manifests/ — pull rendered URL during asset-process step.
Brand direction: Cerkl brand colors per design CONTEXT. Logo placement per speaker-card template. Headshot at left, type block at right. Date/time legible at mobile feed size.

Summary: Social Media - LinkedIn - Link post: How to Claim Your Strategic Seat in Internal Communications
Description:
Topic: Link post pointing to the strategic-seat blog
Post type: static-blog
Wraps: claim-strategic-seat-internal-communications
Copy:
[CAPTION]

"Be more strategic" is the most useless advice an IC manager gets from their CHRO. It names the thing without telling you how.

There are five decisions IC should be in the room for in 2026: AI rollout and policy, change communications for M&A or restructuring, crisis comms, the employee value proposition, and cross-functional measurement of engagement and retention signals. Each one has a version where IC is consulted late, and a version where IC is the architect.

Four specific conversations move you from the first version to the second. The new blog walks through the language to use, the metrics to bring, and the executive-vocabulary upgrade that gets IC pulled into the strategy meeting earlier.

For IC managers and directors caught between AI taking the tactical work and not being in the leadership conversation yet, this is a practitioner's playbook, not a pep talk.

Full breakdown:

https://cerkl.com/blog/claim-strategic-seat-internal-communications
